text:
The purpose of this investigation was to analyse temporally changes in participation rates, performance indices, and peak performance age for the ‘Ultraman Hawaii’ (UH) event over a sixteen year period, 1983-1999 with supplementary data for the years 2000-2012. Information on the participation of ultradistance athletes in multistage ultra-triathlon events is limited; the ‘Ironman Hawaii’ distance single stage ultra-triathlon has been the focus of a number of recent investigations, and indeed most ultratriathlon research has addressed this event alone (e.g., Moseley et al. 1983, 1989, 1995; DeBold & Stembridge 1990). Only a small number of races, including ‘Ultraman Hawaii’, provide a multidisciplinary metric over the three disciplines of swim, bike, and run. In total 570 males and 98 female finishers of the 515 km event in three days of competition (average overall velocity <4km•h-1, race duration of some 108hr) comprised the data base from which total race time and split times for the individual events of 10km (swim), 421km (bike) and 84km (run) were obtained.

The female to male participation ratio significantly increased over the thirty year duration of the study while male participation levels remained unchanged. The performances of the annual winners and medallists for both sexes also showed a highly significant increase. The sex performance differential (SPD) which originally measured 24.3% increased in the mid-1990’s before decreasing to 11.5% by 2012. Detailed discipline specific analysis indicated that improvements occurred in both the cycling sections of Day 1 and Day 2 while there was no change in swimming and running performances.

We have also reported an increase in the age of annual winners. For men, the winners’ age increased from 28 to 47 years from 1966 to 2015, with little change in the winners’ age for women, though there was a large increase in the average age of the top three finishers for women. These findings suggest that athletes are reaching peak performance at older ages in multi-stage ultra-endurance events, and performance gap between the sexes is narrowing to a level more comparable to other endurance activities. Future studies are necessary to better understand the physiological and strategy factors involved in such multi-day competitions.
